content.problemStatement:
C++ exception classes are a set of standard exception types provided by the C++ Standard Library to represent different types of errors and exceptional situations that can occur during program execution. These classes are part of the stdexcept header.

Here are some commonly used C++ exception classes:

- exception: The base class for all C++ standard library exceptions. It defines a what() member function that returns a C-style string describing the exception.

- runtime_error: Represents errors that can be detected during runtime. It is often used for exceptions that are not related to logic errors but rather to unexpected conditions.

- logic_error: Represents logic errors that should be caught at compile time but may be detected at runtime. Examples include attempting to access an out-of-range element in a container or using an invalid argument in a function

- out_of_range: Represents exceptions when an attempt is made to access an element outside the valid range of a container or array.

- overflow_error: Represents exceptions related to arithmetic operations that result in an overflow, such as exceeding the maximum representable value of an integer type.

- underflow_error: Used for exceptions related to arithmetic operations that result in an underflow, such as going below the minimum representable value of an integer type.

- invalid_argument: Represents exceptions that occur when an invalid argument is passed to a function, such as a function that expects a non-empty string but receives an empty one.

- bad_alloc: Thrown by the new operator when it fails to allocate memory using new. This usually indicates that the system is out of memory.

- bad_cast: Thrown by dynamic_cast when an object of a polymorphic type is cast to a reference type of an incorrect dynamic type.

- bad_typeid: Thrown by the typeid operator when it is applied to a null pointer.

- domain_error: Thrown when a function receives an argument that is outside of the expected domain.

- length_error: Thrown when an operation exceeds a predefined limit on the length of a data structure.

- range_error: Thrown when a mathematical operation exceeds the representational limits of the result type.

- system_error: Represents errors reported by the operating system or other low-level C libraries. It's often used with error codes from the  header.

These exception classes provide a structured way to handle different types of errors in your C++ code.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
